revision.comment: [[zh:豐田神社]]の 2023年5月4日 (四) 03:34‎の版から翻訳して作成
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=豊田神社 |画像= |所在地=[[花蓮港庁]]{{仮リンク|花蓮郡|zh|花蓮郡}}{{仮リンク|寿庄|zh|寿庄}}豊田村森本（現在の[[花蓮県]][[寿豊郷]]豊裡村民権街と中山路交差点および民権街と豊正路交差点{{r|文}} |祭神=[[能久親王]]
[[大国魂命]]
[[大己貴命]]
[[少彦名命]]{{r|社}} |社格= 無格社{{r|社}} |創建=[[1915年]]（[[大正]]4年） | 緯度度 =23 | 緯度分 =50 | 緯度秒 =19.79 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N 　 | 経度度 =121 |経度分 =30 | 経度秒 =1.01 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | ISO = TW |地図 = Taiwan |ラベル位置 = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|碧蓮寺]] [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|豊田神社参道と遺構]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|碧蓮寺]] '''豊田神社'''（とよたじんじゃ）は、[[日本統治時代の台湾]]における{{仮リンク|台湾の官営日本人移民村|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}}のひとつ、[[豊田村 (花蓮港庁)|豊田村]]（現在の[[台湾]][[花蓮県]][[寿豊郷]]豊裡、豊山、豊坪など3集落にわたる地域）に存在した神社である。1915年に建立され、[[第2次世界大戦]]後は'''碧蓮寺'''に改装された{{r|潘}}。神社遺跡の[[鳥居]]、石燈籠と狛犬などは2009年8月13日をもって「豊田神社参道と遺構（碧蓮寺の周辺施設）」の名義で{{仮リンク|花蓮県歴史建築|zh|花蓮県歷史建築}}に指定された{{r|文}}。 == 沿革 == === 日本統治時代 === 豊田村は日本統治時代の台湾における日本人移民村の一つで、1913年に開基した。翌年の1914年10月、村の代表者である上野二三、金子義敦、石内伝三郎に小松兼太郎らが[[台湾総督府]]に神社設立を申請した。神社は豊田村の大平、中里、森本、山下集落の日本人住民の精神的支柱としての存在が求められた{{Cite web|title=豊田神社建設許可|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=臺灣總督府|date=1914|format=|accessdate= 2023-12-09|publisher=臺灣總督府檔案|language=}}。豊田神社は大正3年（1914年）12月に建築が開始され、翌年1月に本殿が完成、拝殿と鳥居、6月5日に鎮座式が挙行された。例祭日ははじめ鎮座式の6月5日と定められたが、この時期は農繁期でなおかつ炎熱の気候にさいなまれるため大正11年（1922年）に10月25日に改められた{{r|潘}}。豊田神社は後の1937年6月5日、改築を祝っての遷座式が改めて執り行われた{{Cite journal|title=豐田神社祭|author=臺灣農林新聞社|url=|journal=臺灣農林新聞|issue=18|doi=|others=|year=1937-06-23|volume=|page=|pmid=}}。 File:豐田神社1.jpg|豊田神社鳥居と参道 File:豐田神社2.jpg|豊田神社拝殿および本殿 === 戦後 === 第2次世界大戦後も豊田神社の建築は残され、民国35年（[[1946年]]）年に[[釈迦]]および[[不動明王]]を祀る「豊田碧蓮寺」に改められ、[[灌仏会]]、[[中元節]]、{{仮リンク|下元節|zh|下元節}}の折は祭礼が執り行われた{{r|內}}。だが民国47年（[[1958年]]）7月、{{仮リンク|ウイニー台風|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}}の襲来によって神社以来の建築は倒壊し、住民は仮小屋を設けて釈迦と不動明王の像を守った。そして民国50年（[[1961年]]）、[[宜蘭県]][[羅東鎮]]の東山寺に新築された大殿を参考として再建工事が進み翌年3月に落成、名称は正式に「豊田碧蓮寺」と改められ、新たに[[神農]]、[[后土|地母娘宮]]、[[媽祖]]が祀られた{{r|內}}。 == 神職 == {| class="wikitable" |+豊田神社歴代神職一覽{{Cite web|title=臺灣總督府職員錄系統|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|author=|date=|format=|accessdate= 2023-12-09|publisher=中央研究院臺灣史研究所|language=|archive-date=2019-07-13|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190713180357/http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|dead-url=no}} !姓名 !任期 !備考 |- |小松兼太郎 |1930年から1937年 |1930年から豊田神社の神職を勤めるが、任期中に死去した{{Cite journal|title=彙報 神職死亡|author=臺灣總督府|url=|journal=臺灣總督府報|issue=|doi=|others=|year=1937-09-10|volume=|page=|pmid=}} |- |立川虎之助 |1938年至1942年 |[[林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|林田神社]]の神職を兼任{{Cite journal|title=敘任及辭令|author=臺灣總督府|url=|journal=臺灣總督府報|issue=|doi=|others=|year=1938-04-05|volume=|page=|pmid=}}、のちに依頼退職。 |- |小松石夫 |1942年から1945年 |小松兼太郎の子{{Cite book|title=後山來去: 豐田村誌|last=楊|first=富民|publisher=大塊文化|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400|location=|pages=}} |} == 建築 == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|豊田神社鳥居]] 豊田神社は北向き（実際には北東向き）の建築である。他の台湾の神社が南向きであることから見れば珍しい。かつての神社の拝殿の位置に碧蓮寺が建てられ、豊裡村中山路と民権街の交差点に寺の山門の意味合いを持つ鳥居がある。一の鳥居から寺院の敷地まで数百メートルにわたる南西向きの道のりはかつての参道であり、碧蓮寺に改装された現在でも石灯籠が配置され狛犬が鎮座している。境内には[[台湾総督]]・[[長谷川清]]の揮毫による「開村三十週年紀念碑」が現存する{{r|潘}}。 == 祭神 == 豊田神社の祭神は[[大国魂命]]、[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[能久親王]]だった。碧蓮寺に改められてのちは[[釈迦]]、[[不動明王]]、[[神農|五穀爺]]、[[后土|地母娘娘]]、[[媽祖|天上聖母]]、[[関羽|関聖帝君]]と、[[仏教]]と[[道教]]が同居している。 == 関連項目 == * [[台湾の神社]] * [[林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|林田神社]] * [[吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|吉野神社]] == 参考文献 == {{reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=臺灣總督府社會課編|title=《臺灣に於ける神社及宗教》|language=ja|publisher=臺灣總督府文教局社會課|year=1943|pages=18頁}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=潘繼道 |title=〈花蓮豐田村日治時期遺跡（上）——豐田神社〉|journal=《臺灣文獻別冊》1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=國史館臺灣文獻館 |pages=48─53頁}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=豐田神社參道與遺構(碧蓮寺之週邊設施) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=文化部文化資產局 |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=內政部全國宗教資訊系統——碧蓮寺歷史沿革|accessdate=2013-08-24}}{{dead link|date=2018年1月 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:とよたしんしや}} [[Category:台湾の神社]] [[Category:現存しない台湾の建築物]] [[Category:花蓮県の歴史]]

2023-12-09|publisher=臺灣總督府檔案|language=}}。豊田神社は大正3年（1914年）12月に建築が開始され、翌年1月に本殿が完成、拝殿と鳥居、6月5日に鎮座式が挙行された。例祭日ははじめ鎮座式の6月5日と定められたが、この時期は農繁期でなおかつ炎熱の気候にさいなまれるため大正11年（1922年）に10月25日に改められた{{r|潘}}。豊田神社は後の1937年6月5日、改築を祝っての遷座式が改めて執り行われた{{Cite }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|碧蓮寺]] [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|豊田神社参道と遺構]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|碧蓮寺]] '''豊田神社'''（とよたじんじゃ）は、[[日本統治時代の台湾]]における{{仮リンク|台湾の官営日本人移民村|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}}のひとつ、{{仮リンク|豊田村 (花蓮港庁)|label=豊田村|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}}（現在の[[台湾]][[花蓮県]][[寿豊郷]]豊裡、豊山、豊坪など3集落にわたる地域）に存在した神社である。1915年に建立され、[[第2次世界大戦]]後は'''碧蓮寺'''に改装された{{r|潘}}。神社遺跡の[[鳥居]]、石燈籠と狛犬などは2009年8月13日をもって「豊田神社参道と遺構（碧蓮寺の周辺施設）」の名義で{{仮リンク|花蓮県歴史建築|zh|花蓮県歷史建築}}に指定された{{r|文}}。 == 沿革 == === 日本統治時代 === {{仮リンク|豊田村 (花蓮港庁)|label=豊田村|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}}は日本統治時代の台湾における日本人移民村の一つで、1913年に開基した。翌年の1914年10月、村の代表者である上野二三、金子義敦、石内伝三郎に小松兼太郎らが[[台湾総督府]]に神社設立を申請した。神社は豊田村の大平、中里、森本、山下集落の日本人住民の精神的支柱としての存在が求められた{{Cite web|title=豊田神社建設許可|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=臺灣總督府|date=1914|format=|accessdate= 2023-12-09|publisher=臺灣總督府檔案|language=}}。豊田神社は大正3年（1914年）12月に建築が開始され、翌年1月に本殿が完成、拝殿と鳥居、6月5日に鎮座式が挙行された。例祭日ははじめ鎮座式の6月5日と定められたが、この時期は農繁期でなおかつ炎熱の気候にさいなまれるため大正11年（1922年）に10月25日に改められた{{r|潘}}。豊田神社は後の1937年6月5日、改築を祝っての遷座式が改めて執り行われた{{Cite journal|title=豐田神社祭|author=臺灣農林新聞社|url=|journal=臺灣農林新聞|issue=18|doi=|others=|year=1937-06-23|volume=|page=|pmid=}}。 File:豐田神社1.jpg|豊田神社鳥居と参道 File:豐田神社2.jpg|豊田神社拝殿および本殿 === 戦後 === 第2次世界大戦後も豊田神社の建築は残され、民国35年（[[1946年]]）年に[[釈迦]]および[[不動明王]]を祀る「豊田碧蓮寺」に改められ、[[灌仏会]]、[[中元節]]、{{仮リンク|下元節|zh|下元節}}の折は祭礼が執り行われた{{r|內}}。だが民国47年（[[1958年]]）7月、{{仮リンク|ウイニー台風|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}}の襲来によって神社以来の建築は倒壊し、住民は仮小屋を設けて釈迦と不動明王の像を守った。そして民国50年（[[1961年]]）、[[宜蘭県]][[羅東鎮]]の東山寺に新築された大殿を参考として再建工事が進み翌年3月に落成、名称は正式に「豊田碧蓮寺」と改められ、新たに[[神農]]、[[后土|地母娘宮]]、[[媽祖]]が祀られた{{r|內}}。 == 神職 == {| class="wikitable" |+豊田神社歴代神職一覽{{Cite web|title=臺灣總督府職員錄系統|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|author=|date=|format=|accessdate= 2023-12-09|publisher=中央研究院臺灣史研究所|language=|archive-date=2019-07-13|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190713180357/http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|dead-url=no}} !姓名 !任期 !備考 |- |小松兼太郎 |1930年から1937年 |1930年から豊田神社の神職を勤めるが、任期中に死去した{{Cite journal|title=彙報 神職死亡|author=臺灣總督府|url=|journal=臺灣總督府報|issue=|doi=|others=|year=1937-09-10|volume=|page=|pmid=}} |- |立川虎之助 |1938年至1942年 |[[林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|林田神社]]の神職を兼任{{Cite journal|title=敘任及辭令|author=臺灣總督府|url=|journal=臺灣總督府報|issue=|doi=|others=|year=1938-04-05|volume=|page=|pmid=}}、のちに依願退職。 |- |小松石夫 |1942年から1945年 |小松兼太郎の子{{Cite book|title=後山來去: 豐田村誌|last=楊|first=富民|publisher=大塊文化|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400|location=|pages=}} |} == 建築 == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|豊田神社鳥居]] 豊田神社は北向き（実際には北東向き）の建築である。他の台湾の神社が南向きであることから見れば珍しい。かつての神社の拝殿の位置に碧蓮寺が建てられ、豊裡村中山路と民権街の交差点に寺の山門の意味合いを持つ鳥居がある。一の鳥居から寺院の敷地まで数百メートルにわたる南西向きの道のりはかつての参道であり、碧蓮寺に改装された現在でも石灯籠が配置され狛犬が鎮座している。境内には[[台湾総督]]・[[長谷川清]]の揮毫による「開村三十週年紀念碑」が現存する{{r|潘}}。 == 祭神 == 豊田神社の祭神は[[大国魂命]]、[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[能久親王]]だった。碧蓮寺に改められてのちは[[釈迦]]、[[不動明王]]、[[神農|五穀爺]]、[[后土|地母娘娘]]、[[媽祖|天上聖母]]、[[関羽|関聖帝君]]と、[[仏教]]と[[道教]]が同居している。 == 関連項目 == * [[台湾の神社]] * [[林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|林田神社]] * [[吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|吉野神社]] == 参考文献 == {{reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=臺灣總督府社會課編|title=《臺灣に於ける神社及宗教》|language=ja|publisher=臺灣總督府文教局社會課|year=1943|pages=18頁}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=潘繼道 |title=〈花蓮豐田村日治時期遺跡（上）——豐田神社〉|journal=《臺灣文獻別冊》1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=國史館臺灣文獻館 |pages=48─53頁}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=豐田神社參道與遺構(碧蓮寺之週邊設施) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=文化部文化資產局 |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=內政部全國宗教資訊系統——碧蓮寺歷史沿革|accessdate=2013-08-24}}{{dead link|date=2018年1月 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:とよたしんしや}} [[Category:台湾の神社]] [[Category:現存しない台湾の建築物]] [[Category:花蓮県の歴史]]

{{Shinto Shrine | Name = Toyota Shrine | Image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | Location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | Coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | Enshrined Deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine Rank = Ungraded Shrine | Founded = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! Name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota {{Shinto Shrine | Name = Toyota Shrine | Image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | Location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | Coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | Enshrined Deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine Rank = Ungraded Shrine | Founded = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! Name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[Category:kk]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:mass bump]] Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/豊田神社]]

Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota [[Category:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port {{Shinto Shrine | Name = Toyota Shrine | Image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | Location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | Coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | Enshrined Deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | Shrine Rank = Ungraded Shrine | Founded = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! Name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! Name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines {{Shinto Shrine | Name = Toyota Shrine | image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecrated = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[Category:kk]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/豊田神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[Category:kk]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/豊田神社]]

== Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/豊田神社]]

Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)-qq]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)-qq]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]]
