{{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota {{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota {{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)-qq]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)-qq]] Government)]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)-qq]] {{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines {{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines {{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities {{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine]][[category:kk]] [[Category:Ugoodbye $1 categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]][[category:qq close]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien (花蓮港庁)]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] off this bad category]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:tier 2]] [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota {{Shinto Shrine | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = {{ill|Hualien Prefecture|ja|花蓮港庁}}{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷}}, {{ill|Hualien County|ja|花蓮県}}, encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Defunct buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:Hualien County history]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyota Shrine}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Toyota Shrine (Hualien (花蓮港庁)]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:images]] [[Category:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Toyota Shrine (Hualien Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the {{ill|Taiwan Governor-General's Office|ja|台湾総督府}} for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} and {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}. Festivals were held for {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|ja|灌仏会}}, {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong Township|ja|羅東鎮}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}}, and {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Hasegawa Kiyoshi|ja|長谷川清}}, then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * {{ill|Shakyamuni|ja|釈迦}} * {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}} * {{ill|Shennong|ja|神農}} * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母}} * {{ill|Mazu|ja|媽祖}} * {{ill|Guan Yu|ja|関羽}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto shrines in Taiwan|ja|台湾の神社}} * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines
