[[sn:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q15914360]] Wikidata name: Bilian Temple [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = [[:en:Karenkō Prefecture|Hualien Prefecture]]{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|qq=}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|qq=}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the [[:en:Government-General of Taiwan|Taiwan Governor-General's Office]] for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] and [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Festivals were held for [[:en:Buddha's Birthday|Buddha's Birthday]], {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongyuan Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiayuan Festival|qq=}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winnie Typhoon|qq=}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in [[:en:Luodong|Luodong Township]], and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]], {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]]. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917397}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in [[:en:Luodong|Luodong Township]], and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]], {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]]. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino [[sn:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q15914360]] Wikidata name: Bilian Temple [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = [[:en:Karenkō Prefecture|Hualien Prefecture]]{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|qq=}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|qq=}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the [[:en:Government-General of Taiwan|Taiwan Governor-General's Office]] for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] and [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Festivals were held for [[:en:Buddha's Birthday|Buddha's Birthday]], {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongyuan Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiayuan Festival|qq=}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winnie Typhoon|qq=}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in [[:en:Luodong|Luodong Township]], and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]], {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]]. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917397}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{translated page|ja|豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=103142177|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] [[Category:Former Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917397}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{translated page|ja|豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=103142177|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] [[Category:Former [[commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q15914360]] Wikidata name: Bilian Temple [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = [[:en:Karenkō Prefecture|Hualien Prefecture]]{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|qq=}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|qq=}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the [[:en:Government-General of Taiwan|Taiwan Governor-General's Office]] for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] and [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Festivals were held for [[:en:Buddha's Birthday|Buddha's Birthday]], {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongyuan Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiayuan Festival|qq=}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winnie Typhoon|qq=}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in [[:en:Luodong|Luodong Township]], and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]], {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]]. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917397}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{translated page|ja|豊田神社 Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917397}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q15914360]] Wikidata name: Bilian Temple [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = [[:en:Karenkō Prefecture|Hualien Prefecture]]{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|qq=}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|qq=}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the [[:en:Government-General of Taiwan|Taiwan Governor-General's Office]] for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] and [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Festivals were held for [[:en:Buddha's Birthday|Buddha's Birthday]], {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongyuan Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiayuan Festival|qq=}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winnie Typhoon|qq=}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in [[:en:Luodong|Luodong Township]], and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]], {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]]. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917397}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{translated page|ja|豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=103142177|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] {{Commons category|Bilian Temple}} [[zh:豐田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q15914360}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q15914360]] Wikidata name: Bilian Temple [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = [[:en:Karenkō Prefecture|Hualien Prefecture]]{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|qq=}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|qq=}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the [[:en:Government-General of Taiwan|Taiwan Governor-General's Office]] for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] and [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Festivals were held for [[:en:Buddha's Birthday|Buddha's Birthday]], {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongyuan Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiayuan Festival|qq=}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winnie Typhoon|qq=}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in [[:en:Luodong|Luodong Township]], and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]], {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]]. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{translated page|ja|豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=103142177|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] {{Commons category|Bilian Temple}} [[zh:豐田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q15914360}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q15914360]] Wikidata name: Bilian Temple [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = [[:en:Karenkō Prefecture|Hualien Prefecture]]{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|qq=}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|qq=}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the [[:en:Government-General of Taiwan|Taiwan Governor-General's Office]] for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] and [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Festivals were held for [[:en:Buddha's Birthday|Buddha's Birthday]], {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongyuan Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiayuan Festival|qq=}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winnie Typhoon|qq=}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in [[:en:Luodong|Luodong Township]], and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]], {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]]. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917397}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{translated page|ja|豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=103142177|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] {{Commons category|Bilian Temple}} [[zh:豐田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q15914360}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] revision.comment: Text replacement - "Toyota Shrine (Hualien Port Government)" to "Toyota Shrine (Karenkō Prefecture)"
revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Bilian Temple]][[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]][[zh:豐田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q15914360]] Wikidata name: Bilian Temple [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | name = Toyota Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Bilian Temple, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|250px]] | location = [[:en:Karenkō Prefecture|Hualien Prefecture]]{{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, Taiwan (intersection of Minquan Street and Zhongshan Road in present-day [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], Taiwan) | coordinates = {{Coord|23|50|19.79|N|121|30|1.01|E|region:TW_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Toyota Shrine}} | deity = {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}}
{{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}}
{{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}
{{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} | type = Ungraded Shrine | consecration_year = 1915 (Taishō 4) | Main Hall Style = | Map = Taiwan | Label Position = left }} [[File:Bilian Temple, Shoufeng Township, Hualien (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|Toyota Shrine approach and remnants]] [[File:Fengtian Shinto Shrine (Bilian Temple), Main Shrine (Taiwan).jpg|thumb|right|Bilian Temple]] '''Toyota Shrine''' (豊田神社) was a Shinto shrine located in one of the {{ill|Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|zh|台灣日治時期日本移民村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiwanese Japanese settlement villages|qq=}} during the Japanese colonial period of Taiwan. It was situated in the {{ill|Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|zh|豐田村 (花蓮港廳)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyota Village (Hualien Prefecture)|qq=}} area, which is now part of {{ill|Shoufeng Township|zh|壽豊郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoufeng Township|qq=}}, [[:en:Hualien County|Hualien County]], encompassing the regions of Fuli, Fushan, and Fuping. Established in 1915, the shrine was later converted into '''Bilian Temple''' following World War II. == History == === Japanese Colonial Period === Toyota Village was one of the Japanese settlement villages in Taiwan, established in 1913. In October 1914, representatives of the village—Ueno Niji, Kaneko Yoshinobu, and Ishiuchi Densaburō—along with Komatsu Kentarō, applied to the [[:en:Government-General of Taiwan|Taiwan Governor-General's Office]] for permission to establish the shrine. It was intended to serve as a spiritual center for the Japanese residents of Toyota Village, including Ōhira, Nakazato, Morimoto, and Yamashita hamlets.{{Cite web|title=Application for Toyota Shrine Construction|url=http://ds3.th.gov.tw/ds3/app000/list3.php?ID1=00002252007|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|date=1914|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General Archives|accessdate=2023-12-09}} Construction began in December 1914 (Taishō 3), and the honden (main hall) was completed in January of the following year, with the haiden (worship hall) and torii following shortly after. The enshrinement ceremony took place on June 5, 1915. Initially, the annual festival was set for this date, but due to the agricultural peak season and extreme heat, it was moved to October 25 in 1922 (Taishō 11). In 1937, the shrine was renovated, and a relocation ceremony was held on June 5 to commemorate the reconstruction.{{Cite journal|title=Toyota Shrine Festival|author=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News Agency|journal=Taiwan Agriculture and Forestry News|issue=18|date=1937-06-23}} File:豐田神社1.jpg|Torii and approach of Toyota Shrine File:豐田神社2.jpg|Haiden and honden of Toyota Shrine === Post-War Period === After World War II, the shrine’s structures remained intact and were repurposed in 1946 as "Toyota Bilian Temple," dedicated to [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] and [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]]. Festivals were held for [[:en:Buddha's Birthday|Buddha's Birthday]], {{ill|Zhongyuan Festival|ja|中元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongyuan Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}}, and {{ill|Xiayuan Festival|zh|下元節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiayuan Festival|qq=}}. However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winnie Typhoon|qq=}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in [[:en:Luodong|Luodong Township]], and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]], {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]]. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! Term !! Notes |- | Komatsu Kentarō || 1930–1937 || Served as chief priest until his death during his term.{{Cite journal|title=Obituary: Shrine Priest Death|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1937-09-10}} |- | Tachikawa Toranosuke || 1938–1942 || Concurrently served as a priest at {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}}.{{Cite journal|title=Appointments and Resignations|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office|journal=Taiwan Governor-General Gazette|date=1938-04-05}} |- | Komatsu Ishiō || 1942–1945 || Son of Komatsu Kentarō.{{Cite book|title=Back and Forth in Houzan: Toyota Village Records|author=Yang Fumin|publisher=Great Block Publishing|year=2019|isbn=9789865406400}} |} == Architecture == [[File:Torii of Toyota Jinjya in Hualien, Taiwan.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii of Toyota Shrine]] Toyota Shrine faced north (northeast), a unique orientation compared to most south-facing shrines in Taiwan. Bilian Temple was built on the original site of the shrine’s haiden, and the torii at the intersection of Zhongshan Road and Minquan Street now serves as the temple’s entrance. The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by [[:en:Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|Hasegawa Kiyoshi]], then Governor-General of Taiwan. == Enshrined Deities == Toyota Shrine originally enshrined: * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 国魂}} * {{ill|Ōnamuchi|ja|大己貴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnamuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} * {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} * {{ill|Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja|能久親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 北白川宮能久親王|1=Prince Kitashirakawa Yoshihisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3197569}} After its conversion to Bilian Temple, additional Buddhist and Taoist deities were added, including: * [[:en:The Buddha|Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] * [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] * [[:en:Shennong|Shennong]] * {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Mazu|Mazu]] * [[:en:Guan Yu|Guan Yu]] == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Taiwan|Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] * {{ill|Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|林田神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashida Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Hayashida Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11106704}} * {{ill|Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|ja|吉野神社 (花蓮港庁)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshino Shrine (Hualien Prefecture)|1=Yoshino Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10917397}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|url=http://jdlib.ntl.gov.tw/cgi-bin/gs/gsweb.cgi?ccd=ik3Dwg&o=v0-6#|author=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|title=Shinto Shrines and Religions in Taiwan|language=ja|publisher=Taiwan Governor-General's Office Social Division|year=1943|pages=18}}{{dead link|date=2017-10 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }} {{cite journal|author=Pan Jidao |title=Toyota Shrine in Hualien (Part 1)|journal=Taiwan Historical Documents Supplement 1 |date=2002-06-30 |publisher=Taiwan Historica |pages=48–53}} {{cite web |url=http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |title=Toyota Shrine Approach and Remains (Bilian Temple Facilities) |accessdate=2013-08-24 |publisher=Cultural Heritage Administration |deadurl=yes |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304141042/http://www.boch.gov.tw/boch/frontsite/cultureassets/caseBasicInfoAction.do?method=doViewCaseBasicInfo&caseId=UA09905000145&version=1&assetsClassifyId=1.2&menuId=310&iscancel=true |archivedate=2016-03-04 }} {{cite web|url=http://religion.moi.gov.tw/web/04-data.aspx?tag=TP&id=U974010103011&part=H|title=History of Bilian Temple|accessdate=2013-08-24}} }} {{translated page|ja|豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)|version=103142177|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:豊田神社 (花蓮港庁)]] [[ja:豊田神社]] {{Commons category|Bilian Temple}} [[zh:豐田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q15914360}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Former buildings in Taiwan]] [[Category:History of Hualien County]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Taiwan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

However, in 1958, the {{ill|Winnie Typhoon|zh|熱帶氣旋溫妮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winnie Typhoon|qq=}} destroyed the remaining structures. Residents erected a temporary shelter to house the images of Shakyamuni Tathāgata and Fudō Myōō. By 1961, reconstruction began, inspired by the Daiden of Dongshan Temple in {{ill|Luodong|en|Luodong|WD=Q706261|lt=Luodong Township}}, and the new temple was completed in 1962. Renamed "Toyota Bilian Temple," the site incorporated additional deities, including {{ill|Shennong|en|Shennong|WD=Q313336|lt=Shennong}}, {{ill|Houtu|ja|地母|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Houtu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Mazu|en|Mazu|WD=Q418226|lt=Mazu}}. == Priests == {| class="wikitable" |+ Toyota Shrine Chief Priests{{Cite web|title=Taiwan Governor-General Personnel Records|url=http://who.ith.sinica.edu.tw/mpView.action|publisher=Institute of Taiwan History, Academia Sinica|accessdate=2023-12-09}} ! name !! The former approach, several hundred meters southwest, retains its original stone lanterns and guardian lion-dog statues. The grounds also feature a commemorative monument celebrating the 30th anniversary of the village, inscribed by {{ill|Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|en|Kiyoshi Hasegawa (admiral)|WD=Q5353732|lt=Hasegawa
